sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old male with a history of diabetes type two and mild intermittent asthma, presents with a 3-week history of right elbow pain.

He reports that the pain began after increasing his tennis activity, playing at a country club, and describes the pain as a sharp, stabbing, and aching pain on the outside part of his elbow, rated as an 8/10 when using his arm and 3/10 when resting.
He denies any trauma or recent falls.
He has tried taking Tylenol and ibuprofen, but they do not provide significant relief.
He also reports having tried icing the area, but it did not help.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes type two, managed with metformin 500mg twice a day, and mild intermittent asthma, well-controlled with flovent and albuterol as needed.
He has a history of penicillin allergy and underwent cholecystectomy 10 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient reports no other symptoms.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes
Flovent as needed for asthma
Albuterol as needed for asthma
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient has moderate lateral epicondylar tenderness of the right elbow, with pain on passive range of motion.
There is no palpable joint effusion.
The patient has mild pain with resisted extension of the right wrist.
Test Results:
Right elbow x-ray: No acute fracture or other bony abnormality, no malalignment or joint effusion.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow, also known as tennis elbow, likely due to overuse of the elbow joint.
Problem list:
Acute lateral epicondylitis of the right elbow; diabetes type two; mild intermittent asthma.
Plan:
I prescribed ultram 50mg once per day for pain relief.
I advised the patient to rest his right arm, stop tennis for 4-6 weeks, and avoid repetitive stress on the right arm.
I recommended icing the right elbow for 20 minutes, 3 times a day, and provided a brace for the right forearm to help with discomfort.
I will see the patient in 4 weeks to reevaluate the elbow and consider additional imaging or physical therapy if needed.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient in 4 weeks to reevaluate the elbow and adjust the treatment plan as needed.
